title: Modules and imports

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Modules and imports; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: The Python Tutorial; Python 3.14; Tutorial chapter 6: modules, packages, import execution, and search path; atomic topic: Modules and imports
sources.claim: Import locates a module through the import system, creates and executes its module object on first load, caches it in sys.modules, and binds selected names in the importer.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Import locates a module through the import system, creates and executes its module object on first load, caches it in sys.modules, and binds selected names in the importer.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A calculation module keeps definitions at top level without command effects, and a CLI imports its public function explicitly. Your goal is to split a script into modules and diagnose a name or import-path failure. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Function contracts and returns; Interpreter and environment. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Import locates a module through the import system, creates and executes its module object on first load, caches it in sys.modules, and binds selected names in the importer. This model is applied through the declared Python: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Circular imports can observe partial initialization, wildcard imports obscure APIs, local names can shadow packages, reload does not rebuild every external reference, and top-level effects run during import. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Import merely pastes definitions and never executes module code, so circular imports cannot expose incomplete state.

includes: Define module APIs and reason about import execution, names and search paths.
Split a script into modules and diagnose a name or import-path failure.
excludes: Circular imports can observe partial initialization, wildcard imports obscure APIs, local names can shadow packages, reload does not rebuild every external reference, and top-level effects run during import.

outcomes: Split a script into modules and diagnose a name or import-path failure.

examples.title: Modules and imports: direct language case
examples.situation: A calculation module keeps definitions at top level without command effects, and a CLI imports its public function explicitly.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Python: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: A calculation module keeps definitions at top level without command effects, and a CLI imports its public function explicitly.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Import executes a module once into a cached module object; keep top-level effects minimal and redesign cycles that depend on partially initialized names.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Import executes a module once into a cached module object; keep top-level effects minimal and redesign cycles that depend on partially initialized names.

examples.title: Modules and imports: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Two modules move a shared protocol into a third module to remove a circular import that read a not-yet-defined name.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Two modules move a shared protocol into a third module to remove a circular import that read a not-yet-defined name.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Circular imports can observe partial initialization, wildcard imports obscure APIs, local names can shadow packages, reload does not rebuild every external reference, and top-level effects run during import.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Import merely pastes definitions and never executes module code, so circular imports cannot expose incomplete state.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Split a script into modules and diagnose a name or import-path failure.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Import merely pastes definitions and never executes module code, so circular imports cannot expose incomplete state.
misconceptions.correction: Import executes a module once into a cached module object; keep top-level effects minimal and redesign cycles that depend on partially initialized names.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Import merely pastes definitions and never executes module code, so circular imports cannot expose incomplete state.

analogy.example: a library whose catalogs, shelves, isolated rooms, and shipping manifest separate import names from installable distributions can introduce Modules and imports, after the canonical Python: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: imports, packages, virtual environments, and pyproject builds
limitations: sys.path, partial module initialization, build backends, editable installs, and wheel contents need direct inspection

recap.summary: You should now be able to split a script into modules and diagnose a name or import-path failure. Keep this boundary in view: Circular imports can observe partial initialization, wildcard imports obscure APIs, local names can shadow packages, reload does not rebuild every external reference, and top-level effects run during import.
